evals.prompt:
Write tests for this LoginForm component. It handles email/password login with validation, API calls, loading states, and error display. The project uses Vitest and React Testing Library. Here's the component:

evals/files/LoginForm.tsx
evals.expected_output: A test file using behavioral testing patterns: getByRole queries, userEvent for interactions, MSW for API mocking, flat test structure with test context or setup helper
expectations: Tests mock the API at the network level using MSW (http.post/http.get with HttpResponse) rather than mocking fetch or the API module with vi.mock
Test structure is flat — no nested describe blocks deeper than 1 level, uses test context (test.extend) or a setup helper function
Test names describe user-facing behavior (e.g., 'shows error when...' or 'redirects after...') not implementation details (e.g., 'calls onSuccess' or 'sets loading state')

evals.prompt:
I need tests for this SearchPage component. It has a debounced search input that calls an API, shows loading/error/empty/results states. Uses the useDebounce hook. Project uses Vitest + React Testing Library. Write comprehensive tests.

evals/files/SearchPage.tsx
evals/files/useDebounce.ts
evals.expected_output: Tests that handle async behavior with debouncing, use fake timers correctly, mock the search API with MSW, and test the full search workflow
expectations: Tests use MSW (http.get with HttpResponse) to mock the /api/search endpoint rather than vi.mock on fetch or the search module
The debounced search workflow is tested as a complete flow (type → wait → see results) rather than testing the useDebounce hook in isolation with renderHook or vi.mock
Fake timer setup/teardown is in test context (test.extend), beforeEach/afterEach, or a setup helper — not done inline in each test body
Timer advancement uses vi.runAllTimersAsync() or vi.runOnlyPendingTimersAsync() rather than vi.advanceTimersByTime with a hardcoded duration

evals.prompt:
Write tests for these cart utility functions. Pure TypeScript functions for calculating subtotals, tax, totals, discounts, and currency formatting. Project uses Vitest.

evals/files/cart-utils.ts
evals.expected_output: Unit tests split by concept across multiple test files, using test.each for parameterized cases
expectations: Uses test.each or similar parameterized pattern for testing multiple inputs against expected outputs rather than writing many separate identical test structures
Tests are split into multiple test files by concept (e.g., one per function or per related group) rather than one monolithic test file with describe blocks as file separators

evals.prompt:
Write tests for the UserSettings component. It loads user profile data from an API on mount, lets the user edit their name/bio and notification preferences, then saves via PUT. It handles loading, save success, and save error states. Project uses Vitest and React Testing Library.

evals/files/UserSettings.tsx
evals.expected_output: Integration tests that render the component, wait for data to load from MSW, interact with form fields and checkboxes, save, and verify success/error messages
expectations: Tests use MSW to mock both GET /api/user/profile (initial load) and PUT /api/user/profile (save) endpoints rather than mocking fetch or internal modules
Tests verify the loading state transitions: shows loading initially, then shows the form with loaded data — in the same test, not split across separate tests
The complete edit-and-save workflow is tested in a single test (load → edit fields → save → see success message) rather than splitting into many tiny tests
Tests verify the PUT request body contains the correct updated data (captured via MSW handler) rather than asserting on component internal state

evals.prompt:
Write tests for this Tooltip component. It shows a tooltip on hover/focus with a configurable delay and position. The tooltip uses getBoundingClientRect for positioning and CSS for visibility. The project uses Vitest browser mode with the Playwright provider. Here's the component:

evals/files/Tooltip.tsx
evals.expected_output: Browser-mode tests using vitest-browser-react render, Locator API for interactions, expect.element() for assertions, testing hover/focus behavior with real browser rendering and proper timer handling
expectations: Timer setup (vi.useFakeTimers) is in beforeEach or test context, not inline in each test body, and timer teardown (vi.useRealTimers) is in afterEach or test context
Timer advancement uses vi.runAllTimersAsync() or vi.runOnlyPendingTimersAsync() rather than vi.advanceTimersByTime with a hardcoded delay value — and timers are NOT sidestepped by passing delay:0
Assertions use expect.element() with toBeVisible() for visible elements rather than toBeInTheDocument()

evals.prompt:
Write Playwright E2E tests for a checkout flow described in this specification. The app has product listing, cart, and checkout pages. Tests should cover the happy path from adding a product to order confirmation. The project already has Playwright configured.

evals/files/checkout-flow.e2e-description.md
evals.expected_output: Playwright E2E tests using page objects combined with fixtures for clean architecture, API seeding for test data, accessible locators, and focused tests on the critical checkout path
expectations: Uses Playwright fixtures (test.extend) for authentication setup rather than clicking through login in each test
Test data is seeded via the /api/test/seed endpoint (direct API call) rather than navigating through UI to create test state
Uses page object classes combined with fixtures — page objects define locators and actions, fixtures inject them into tests via test.extend
The checkout flow is tested as a single end-to-end test with multiple assertions rather than split into disconnected steps
